Exploring AI Use Cases and Applications

Prerequisites
Module 1 — Fundamentals of ML & AI
Leads To
Service selection, deployment, business alignment
Module Introduction

Module 2 bridges theory to practice — mapping AI capabilities to industries, defining the four core application domains, and building a framework for when ML is the right tool and which technique fits. It closes with model selection trade-offs and the business metrics that determine real-world AI success.

A
AI Fundamentals Recap
A
A1Hierarchy and Key Distinction

AI ⊃ ML ⊃ DL ⊃ Generative AI — nested hierarchy. Generative AI creates new content; traditional ML predicts or analyzes existing data. This distinction recurs across every industry use case.

Generative AI content types: Text (conversations, summaries) · Visual (images, video) · Audio (music, speech) · Code (programming outputs).

Generative AI creates · Traditional ML analyzes/predicts — never conflate them
B
Real-World AI Use Cases Across Industries
B
B1Business Value Mechanism

AI creates value through three mechanisms: Automation (replace repetitive manual work) · Optimization (improve decisions beyond human capacity) · Personalization (tailor at scale). [+] These appear differently per industry but the logic is constant.

B2Industry Breakdown
Media & Entertainment
  • Content Generation — scripts, stories
  • Virtual Reality — immersive environments
  • News Generation — auto-summaries
Retail
  • Review Summaries
  • Pricing Optimization
  • Virtual Try-ons
  • Store Layout Optimization
Healthcare
  • HealthScribe — clinical notes
  • Personalized Medicine
  • Medical Imaging Enhancement
Life Sciences
  • Drug Discovery
  • Protein Folding Prediction
  • Synthetic Biology
Financial Services
  • Fraud Detection — AML
  • Portfolio Management
  • Debt Collection Optimization
Manufacturing
  • Predictive Maintenance
  • Process Optimization
  • Product Design Generation
  • Material Science
AWS HealthScribe — automatically generates clinical notes from provider-patient conversations using AI.
6 industries — know ≥2 use cases per industry AWS HealthScribe = clinical documentation AI service
C
Core AI Application Domains
C
C1Computer Vision

Enables machines to interpret visual data (images and video). Three tasks in order of granularity: Image Classification (whole-image label) → Object Detection (locate + label multiple objects) → Segmentation (pixel-level analysis). Use cases: autonomous driving, medical imaging, public safety facial recognition. AWS: Amazon Rekognition.

Classification → Detection → Segmentation (increasing granularity)
C2Natural Language Processing (NLP)

Enables machines to understand and generate human language. Four core tasks: Text Classification · Sentiment Analysis · Translation · Text Generation. Industry use cases: insurance (policy extraction), telecom (recommendations), education (chatbot Q&A). AWS: Comprehend · Translate · Lex.

C3Intelligent Document Processing (IDP)

Applies AI to extract structured information from unstructured documents. Three functions: Data Extraction · Classification · Summarization. IDP is a superset workflow that applies NLP (and CV for scanned images) to document-centric problems. [+] Industry use cases: finance (loan processing), legal (contract automation), healthcare (claims). AWS: Amazon Textract.

C4Fraud Detection

ML models identify anomalous patterns in transaction data or user behavior. Unlike rule-based filters, ML learns what normal looks like and flags deviations — catching novel fraud no rule anticipated. [+] Industry use cases: Finance (AML, transactions) · Retail (account protection) · Telecom (SIM fraud, network monitoring).

4 domains: CV · NLP · IDP · Fraud Detection Each is a category of AI capability, not a single algorithm
D
When to Use ML — and When Not To
D
D1Decision Framework
Use ML When
  • Complex Rules — too many edge cases to define manually (spam filtering)
  • High Variability — many interacting factors (credit scoring)
  • Large Scale — massive datasets, real-time recommendations
Skip ML When
  • Simple Logic — deterministic rules already exist (tax calculation)
  • Low Complexity — predefined steps are sufficient (unit conversion)
ML is not universally better — it is better when rules cannot scale or be defined
Canonical ML case: spam filtering · Non-ML case: basic arithmetic
E
ML Techniques and Their Use Cases
E
E1Supervised Learning

Trains on labeled data to predict outputs for new inputs. Two subtypes:

Classification
Categorical output — one of a fixed set of classes.
  • Fraud detection (fraud / not fraud)
  • Image classification (cat / dog)
  • Customer churn prediction
  • Medical diagnostics
Regression
Continuous output — a numeric value on a scale.
  • Weather forecasting (temperature)
  • Market trend prediction (price)
  • Life expectancy estimation
  • Population growth modeling
Category → Classification · Number → Regression [+]
E2Unsupervised Learning

Discovers hidden structure in unlabeled data — no correct answers provided. Two subtypes:

Clustering
Groups similar data points without predefined categories.
  • Customer segmentation
  • Targeted marketing
  • Recommendation systems
Dimensionality Reduction
Reduces feature count while preserving structure.
  • Data visualization
  • Compression
  • Feature extraction
Unsupervised = no labels — model creates its own structure
E3Reinforcement Learning

Agent learns by interacting with an Environment — receives rewards for correct actions and penalties for incorrect ones, developing a Policy that maximizes cumulative reward. No labeled dataset — learns through trial and error. AWS example: AWS DeepRacer (autonomous race car via RL). [+]

RL components: Agent · Environment · Reward/Penalty · Policy No labels — learns by trial and error over many iterations
F
Capabilities of Generative AI
F
F1The Seven Capabilities
Adaptability
Single FM flexes across different tasks and domains without rebuilding
Responsiveness
Generates output in real time — conversational, instant, live
Simplicity
Automates complex workflows without specialized technical skill
Creativity
Produces genuinely novel outputs — not just template recombinations
Data Efficiency
Adapts to new tasks with limited additional data (pretrained foundation)
Personalization
Tailors outputs to individual users at scale — impossible for rule-based systems
Scalability
Produces content at volume and speed no human team can match
Core value: automation + creativity at scale — simultaneously
G
Challenges and Risks of Generative AI
G
G1The Seven Risks
Hallucinations
Plausible but factually false outputs — model predicts likely text, not correct text. Primary exam risk.
Toxicity
Offensive, harmful, or discriminatory outputs from biased training data or adversarial prompts
Privacy
Models may regenerate personal data from training, or misuse confidential input data
Regulatory
Outputs may expose regulated data (PII, PHI) or generate non-compliant content
Social Risks
Reputationally harmful content, deepfakes, misinformation damaging brand or public trust
Interpretability
Hard to explain why a specific output was produced — complicates audit and compliance
Nondeterminism
Same input → different outputs across runs. Problematic for high-stakes reproducibility needs
Hallucination = plausible but false · AI outputs must always be verified
G2Mitigation Strategies
Guardrails
Filter or block harmful output categories before they reach the user
Verification / RAG
Ground outputs in retrieved factual sources — primary hallucination mitigation
Data Anonymization
Remove or mask PII before training or prompting
Data Curation
Remove harmful or biased content from training data
Model Testing
Evaluate for bias, toxicity, and accuracy before deployment
Monitoring
Continuously detect inconsistencies and output drift in production
Security Measures
Encryption, firewalls, access controls for data protection
Hallucination mitigation: Guardrails + Verification (RAG grounds outputs in facts)
H
Model Selection Factors
H
H1Five Core Factors
Model Type
Does the model support the specific task? Task-capability alignment is the first gate.
Performance
Accuracy and reliability on the target task — measured by benchmarks or human eval
Constraints
Available compute (GPU/CPU/memory), data availability, deployment environment
Compliance
GDPR, HIPAA, ethics, org policies — non-compliant models are disqualified regardless of accuracy
Cost
Larger models = higher accuracy + higher cost. Smaller = cheaper + faster but lower quality.
Compliance is a hard gate — disqualified regardless of performance
H2Foundation Model Examples
AI21 Jurassic-2
Text generation, summarization, Q&A
Amazon Titan
Embeddings, search, Q&A (AWS-native)
Anthropic Claude
Chat, reasoning, coding — long context
Stable Diffusion
Image generation from text prompts
Cohere Command
NLP — classification, summarization
Meta Llama
General text — open-weight, fine-tuning
Model selection = task + cost + performance balance · No universal winner
I
Business Metrics for Generative AI
I
I1Measuring Real-World Success

Technical performance alone is insufficient. AI success = technical performance + business value. Business metrics tell you whether the model actually matters in production. [+]

User Satisfaction
Surveys and ratings — do users find the output helpful and trustworthy?
ARPU
Average Revenue Per User — does AI-driven personalization increase spending?
Cross-Domain Perf.
FM generalization across multiple domains — measures versatility in multi-purpose deployments
Conversion Rate
% of users completing a desired action — direct indicator of AI behavioral impact
Efficiency
Resource and time savings vs. baseline — captures operational ROI
Metrics are use-case specific — no universal success metric Success = technical performance + business value · Both halves required
Module Summary
  • Generative AI creates (text, visual, audio, code); ML analyzes/predicts. AI adds value through automation, optimization, and personalization across 6 industries.
  • Four application domains: CV (classify → detect → segment) · NLP (classify, analyze, translate, generate) · IDP (extract, classify, summarize docs) · Fraud Detection (learn normal, flag deviations).
  • Use ML for complex rules, high variability, large scale. Skip for simple deterministic logic. ML subtypes: Supervised (Classification · Regression) · Unsupervised (Clustering · Dimensionality Reduction) · Reinforcement (Agent → Environment → Reward/Penalty → Policy).
  • Seven Gen AI capabilities: Adaptability · Responsiveness · Simplicity · Creativity · Data Efficiency · Personalization · Scalability. Seven risks: Hallucinations (plausible but false) · Toxicity · Privacy · Regulatory · Social · Interpretability · Nondeterminism. Mitigation: Guardrails, RAG, curation, monitoring.
  • Model selection: 5 factors (Type, Performance, Constraints, Compliance, Cost). Compliance is a hard gate. Six FMs: Jurassic-2, Titan, Claude, Stable Diffusion, Cohere, Llama. Success = technical performance + business metrics (Satisfaction, ARPU, Conversion, Efficiency).
